content.text: Good. Let me read the key architectural docs first:

# Auto Content Factory

Departamento de edicao de video gerido por agentes autonomos.

## O que e isto

A Content Factory hoje produz videos automaticamente atraves de um pipeline deterministico (content_factory_v3). O pipeline funciona вҖ” produz dezenas de videos por dia em multiplos idiomas e contas.

Porem, o pipeline depende 100% do Lucca para:
- Configurar cada conta uma unica vez (prompts, configs, templates, estilos)
- Monitorizar producao e reagir a erros
- Decidir prioridades entre contas/idiomas
- Evoluir qualidade (novos templates, novos estilos, novos prompts)

O Auto Content Factory e a evolucao: uma equipa hierarquica de agentes autonomos que substitui esse papel operacional humano вҖ” tomando decisoes editoriais, crativas e operacionais dentro de limites bem definidos.

## Principio Fundamental

Agentes nao sao codigo. Agentes sao:
- Uma hierarquia de diretorios bem organizada
- Ficheiros markdown claros, completos e conectados
- Cada agente com papel, modelo, ferramentas e limites definidos
- Comunicacao entre agentes via artefactos estruturados (JSON, markdown)

## Relacao com content_factory_v3

O auto_content_factory NAO substitui o content_factory_v3. Ele senta POR CIMA como camada de decisao editorial e operacional. O pipeline deterministico (Python/Remotion) continua sendo o "chao" вҖ” a infraestrutura que executa. Os agentes decidem O QUE executar e COMO.

## Stack Tecnologico

- **Framework de agentes:** OpenClaw (open-source, v2026.3.22+)
- **Orquestracao:** Paperclip (governance, budgets, org charts) вҖ” a avaliar
- **Seguranca:** OpenShell sandboxes (mirror mode nas maquinas Windows)
- **Modelos:** Mix per-agent (Gemini Flash Lite para tarefas simples, modelos maiores para decisoes criativas)
- **Imagem local:** ComfyUI + SDXL/Flux (ja instalados em M1)
- **TTS:** Gemini TTS (free/tier1) + ElevenLabs (PT/ES)
- **Maquinas:** M1-M6 (Windows, RTX GPUs, 128GB RAM)

## Status

- [x] Visao e conceito definidos
- [x] Pesquisa de frameworks (OpenClaw, Paperclip, alternativas)
- [x] OpenClaw instalado no Mac Mini
- [ ] Arquitectura de agentes (branches, hierarquia, responsabilidades)
- [ ] Definicao de modelos/tools/skills por agente
- [ ] Documentacao completa dos agentes
- [ ] Instalacao OpenClaw nas maquinas Windows
- [ ] Configuracao OpenShell/seguranca
- [ ] Implementacao e testes

# Contexto вҖ” Auto Content Factory

## A Tese (Ecossistema de Solucoes)

Documento fundacional criado por Arthur Michielin (Micha) + Lucca, Marco 2026.

### Premissas
1. IA nao serve para ganhar tempo вҖ” serve para LIBERAR tempo
2. Nao replicar processos вҖ” APRIMORAR (agentes fazem melhor que humanos em tarefas repetitivas)
3. SaaS entrega ferramenta ($1). Agent as a Service entrega RESULTADO ($6)
4. Janela de oportunidade: 2025-2030 (antes da AGI consolidar)
5. Content Factory = primeiro case real do ecossistema

### O Ciclo
Aprender -> Aplicar -> Documentar -> Ensinar -> Marketplace

A Content Factory e simultaneamente:
1. O negocio principal (producao de video)
2. O primeiro case de Agents as a Service (documentado como case replicavel)
3. A vitrine do ecossistema de solucoes

## O Pipeline Actual (content_factory_v3)

### Blocos
- **Bloco 1:** Script -> Screenplay -> TTS Audio (per-scene)
- **Bloco 2:** Scene Direction -> Image Prompts -> Image Generation (Gemini/SDXL/Flux)
- **Bloco 3:** GPT Planners -> Python Resolver -> Python TSX Engine -> Remotion Render

### O que ja e automatico
- Pipeline inteiro roda sem intervencao humana uma vez configurado
- 9 contas configuradas (001-013), multiplos idiomas por conta
- Queue CLI para agendar e executar producao
- KMS centralizado para gestao de chaves API
- Fallback automatico entre providers (Gemini free -> tier1 -> OpenRouter)
- 6 maquinas de producao (M1-M6)

### O que ainda depende do Lucca
- Configuracao inicial de cada conta (uma vez)
- Monitorizacao de producao (verificar erros, reagir)
- Decisao de prioridades (qual conta/idioma produzir)
- Evolucao de qualidade (novos templates, prompts, estilos)
- Troubleshooting quando algo falha
- Avaliacao de qualidade dos videos produzidos

## Situacao das APIs (Marco 2026)

### Crise GCP (19-25 Mar 2026)
- Todas as 156 Service Accounts suspensas pelo Google
- Migrado para: 39 API keys tier1 + 37 free + 11 OpenRouter
- Curativo temporario: ~2-3 dias de runway com OpenRouter para imagens (~$5.55/video)
- Decisao: migrar geracao de imagens para modelos locais (SDXL/Flux via ComfyUI)

### Keys Activas
| Tipo | Provider | Tier | Count |
|------|----------|------|-------|
| API Key | Gemini | free | 37 |
| API Key | Gemini | tier1 | 39 |
| API Key | OpenRouter | paid | 11 |
| API Key | OpenAI | pay-as-you-go | 4 |

### Modelos Locais Instalados (M1)
- Juggernaut XL Ragnarok (SDXL, 6.7GB) вҖ” 15s/imagem
- Flux Dev Q6_K (GGUF, 9.2GB) вҖ” 85s/imagem
- LoRAs: FilmGrain Redmond, Monochrome Halsman, Flux Cinematic 1940s

## Progresso Auto Content Factory (25-26 Mar 2026)

### OpenClaw Setup (COMPLETO)
- OpenClaw v2026.3.24 instalado na M1 (Windows nativo)
- Agente "Tobias" criado como CEO do departamento de edicao
- Modelo: GPT-5.4 Pro via ChatGPT Pro OAuth ($200/mo)
- Telegram conectado (@TobiasM1bot)
- Security hardening aplicado (exec ask on-miss, SOUL.md rules)
- 7 ficheiros .md reescritos no workspace (SOUL, IDENTITY, USER, MEMORY, MISSION, CONTEXT, TASKS)
- AGENTS.md boot sequence actualizado

### Arquitectura Definida
- Hierarquia 4 niveis: CEO (Tobias) вҶ’ Production Manager вҶ’ Branch Managers (B1-B10) вҶ’ Agentes (~43)
- Cada nivel workspace isolado no OpenClaw
- Workspace default (Tobias): ~/.openclaw/workspace/
- Sub-agentes: ~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/agent/
- Uma equipa, multiplos clientes (configs por conta)
- Implementacao incremental: 2 вҶ’ 5 вҶ’ 12 вҶ’ 43 agentes

### KnowledgeBase
- 14 aulas Matt Ganzak digeridas (Security, Multi-agent, Skills, Platform, Cron, Sessions, Tokens, SEO, HeyGen, UGC, Prompt Caching, Update, Autonomous SaaS, Mission Control)
- 11 aulas Alex Finn digeridas (What is OpenClaw, Setup, First Things, Mindset, Mission Statement, Mission Control, Morning Brief, Local Models, Telegram, Fix Issues, Vibe Coding)
- Mac Mini workspace analisado e melhores padroes extraidos

### Proximo
1. Instalar Ollama na M1 (modelos locais para sub-agentes)
2. Detalhar Production Manager (primeiro sub-agente)
3. Detalhar branches B1-B10 um a um
4. Configurar Telegram Topics para routing por agente
5. Testar browser automation para img2video

# Arquitectura de Branches вҖ” Departamento de Edicao de Video

## Principio de Design

**Um departamento, multiplos clientes.** A estrutura de agentes e UNICA e compartilhada. O que diferencia cada canal/conta sao os CONFIGS (prompts, style guides, LoRAs, catalogos, templates). Exactamente como uma produtora de video real вҖ” mesma equipa, projectos diferentes.

## Decisoes Tomadas

1. **Uma equipe, multiplos clientes** вҖ” branches compartilhados, configs por conta
2. **Visual Planning ANTES de Asset Research** вҖ” planejar antes de executar (per GPT)
3. **QA distribuido** вҖ” nao so pos-render, verifica em cada etapa
4. **Monitoring separado** вҖ” alertas, custos, erros como branch independente
5. **Scene Bible como artefacto central** вҖ” Branch 1 cria, todos os outros leem
6. **Scheduling/Prioridades FORA** вҖ” pertence ao departamento do Micha
7. **Analytics/Evolucao FORA** вҖ” pertence ao departamento de analise
8. **10 branches, ~42 agentes** вҖ” "mais e melhor, depois retira-se"
9. **Branch 8 quase 100% deterministico** вҖ” agentes nao tocam render/upload
10. **Incremental** вҖ” nao precisamos construir tudo de uma vez

# Estrategia de Modelos вҖ” Auto Content Factory

## Decisao (25 Mar 2026)

### Main Agent (Orquestrador + conversacao)
- **Modelo:** GPT-5.4 via ChatGPT Pro ($200/mo) OAuth
- **Razao:** melhor custo-beneficio para agente principal 24/7, sem billing por token
- **Uso:** conversar com Lucca, delegar tarefas, decisoes de alto nivel

### Sub-agentes (tarefas especificas)
- **Modelos baratos via OpenRouter:** GPT-5.4-mini, GPT-5.4-nano, DeepSeek, MiniMax
- **Anthropic API key ($50 credito inicial):** Claude Haiku, Sonnet, Opus quando necessario
- **Gemini via KMS:** Flash Lite, 2.5 Flash (ja temos 76 keys activas)
- **Modelos locais (Ollama):** para tarefas que nao justificam custo de API

### Principio de Routing
```
Tarefa simples (formatting, parsing, routing)  вҶ’ GPT-5.4-nano / Haiku / Flash Lite
Tarefa media (analise, planning, decisao)      вҶ’ GPT-5.4-mini / Sonnet / 2.5 Flash
Tarefa complexa (direccao criativa, QA)        вҶ’ GPT-5.4 (via Pro OAuth) / Opus (API)
Geracao de imagens                             вҶ’ ComfyUI local (custo zero)
TTS                                            вҶ’ Gemini TTS free (ja temos)
```

|------|-------|
| ChatGPT Pro (main agent) | $200/mo |

### Onboarding do OpenClaw
- Anthropic API key para onboarding inicial ($50 credito)

- Claude fica como opcao para sub-agentes via API key

### Notas
- Throttling do Pro e real mas toleravel para nosso uso (nao somos high-frequency trading)
- Se Plus ($20) funcionar para o volume que precisamos, podemos downgradar depois
- Sempre buscar melhor resultado com melhor custo вҖ” testar modelo mais barato primeiro

# Brain Dump вҖ” OpenClaw Onboarding (Departamento de EdiГ§ГЈo de VГӯdeo)

## Quem somos

Eu sou o Lucca. Sou co-fundador da Content Factory junto com o Micha (Arthur Michielin) e o JoГЈo Gabriel (Cellibs). A Content Factory Г© uma empresa de produГ§ГЈo de vГӯdeo automatizada вҖ” criamos vГӯdeos para YouTube em escala usando inteligГӘncia artificial.

## O que Г© a Content Factory

Temos mГәltiplas contas de YouTube, cada uma com um tema e estilo visual diferente вҖ” histГіria, ciГӘncia, drama, entre outros. Cada conta pode produzir vГӯdeos em vГЎrios idiomas (portuguГӘs, inglГӘs, espanhol, francГӘs, italiano, alemГЈo, e mais). A produГ§ГЈo roda em mГәltiplas mГЎquinas fГӯsicas Windows com GPUs NVIDIA.

## Como funciona a produГ§ГЈo de vГӯdeos HOJE

Hoje a Content Factory jГЎ Г© uma fГЎbrica de vГӯdeos automatizada. Temos um pipeline chamado content_factory_v3 que, uma vez configurado para uma conta, produz vГӯdeos sem intervenГ§ГЈo humana. Mas Г© importante entender o que isso significa e o que NГғO significa.

### O que temos hoje: automaГ§ГЈo "engessada"

O sistema actual funciona assim: para cada conta, um humano (eu, Lucca, junto com o Claude Code) configurou uma Гәnica vez todo o estilo de ediГ§ГЈo вҖ” quais prompts os agentes de IA usam, que tipo de imagens sГЈo geradas, que estilo de lettering aparece, que tipo de mГәsica toca, como as cenas sГЈo compostas. Depois dessa configuraГ§ГЈo, o pipeline gera vГӯdeos automaticamente para diferentes scripts e temas.

Гү como se um editor de vГӯdeo profissional editasse UM vГӯdeo magistralmente, gravasse cada decisГЈo criativa que tomou como um "template", e depois um sistema repetisse aquele estilo para centenas de vГӯdeos diferentes. Cada vГӯdeo Г© Гәnico no CONTEГҡDO (scripts diferentes, personagens diferentes, imagens diferentes), mas o PROCESSO CRIATIVO Г© sempre o mesmo вҖ” os mesmos passos, os mesmos estilos de imagem, os mesmos tipos de composiГ§ГЈo, as mesmas regras de lettering.

Hoje temos agentes de IA dentro do pipeline вҖ” eles dividem cenas, escolhem imagens, planeiam mГәsica. Mas esses agentes seguem instruГ§Гөes fixas definidas nos prompts. NГЈo tomam decisГөes criativas verdadeiras. NГЈo adaptam o estilo baseado no conteГәdo. NГЈo pensam "esta cena pede algo diferente".

### O que queremos construir: ediГ§ГЈo autГіnoma e criativa

O objectivo final Г© ter uma equipa de agentes autГіnomos que funcionem como um time real de editores de vГӯdeo вҖ” onde cada vГӯdeo tem o seu prГіprio processo criativo Гәnico. Agentes que:
- Olham para o script e pensam "esta cena precisa de mais tensГЈo, vou usar mais cortes rГЎpidos"
- Decidem "aqui fica melhor uma foto real do arquivo do que uma imagem gerada por IA"
- Escolhem "este momento pede silГӘncio em vez de mГәsica"
- Adaptam o estilo visual ao conteГәdo especГӯfico de cada vГӯdeo

NГЈo esperamos isso logo de cara. Гү um objectivo a mГ©dio/longo prazo. Mas Г© a direcГ§ГЈo em que estamos a caminhar.

## O pipeline: 3 Blocos

### Bloco 1: Script -> Screenplay -> ГҒudio
Pega um script (texto escrito) e transforma em ГЎudio narrado:
- Divide o script em cenas (cada cena com um nГәmero especГӯfico de palavras por idioma)
- Adapta o texto de cada cena para soar natural quando falado naquele idioma
- Gera voiceover com Gemini Text-to-Speech para todos os idiomas
- Faz transcriГ§ГЈo com WhisperX para obter timestamp de cada palavra (fundamental para sincronizar tudo depois)
- Valida qualidade do ГЎudio (match de palavras, qualidade sonora)
- Resultado: ficheiros MP3 por cena + timestamps exactos de cada palavra

### Bloco 2: DirecГ§ГЈo de Cena -> Imagens
O bloco mais complexo (15 passos de IA). Decide O QUE o espectador vai ver:
- Identifica personagens no script e liga-os a pessoas reais da histГіria
- Planeia o visual de cada cena (mood, composiГ§ГЈo, estilo)
- Procura fotografias reais em arquivos pГәblicos
- Quando nГЈo encontra fotos reais adequadas, gera imagens com IA
- Gera retratos dos personagens para manter consistГӘncia visual
- Resultado: imagens prontas + especificaГ§ГЈo visual completa por cena

### Bloco 3: Montagem -> Render -> VГӯdeo Final
Pega tudo (imagens, ГЎudio, timestamps) e monta o vГӯdeo:
- Planeia mГәsica (que mГәsica, quando, volume, transiГ§Гөes)
- Planeia efeitos sonoros (impactos, atmosfera)
- Planeia texto na tela (lettering: tГӯtulos, ГӘnfases, diГЎlogos)
- Planeia animaГ§Гөes (transiГ§Гөes entre cenas, efeitos visuais, movimentos de cГўmara)
- Resolve nomes de mГәsicas/SFX para ficheiros reais e volumes exactos
- Gera cГіdigo de vГӯdeo em Remotion (framework React/TypeScript)
- Renderiza em 4 partes paralelas e concatena
- Comprime e faz upload
- Resultado: vГӯdeo MP4 final pronto

## Sistema de GestГЈo de Chaves (KMS)

Todas as chamadas de IA do pipeline de produГ§ГЈo passam por um servidor centralizado chamado KMS. Ele gere mais de 90 chaves de API de vГЎrios providers (Gemini, OpenRouter, OpenAI). O KMS decide automaticamente qual chave usar, faz rotaГ§ГЈo quando uma esgota, aplica cooldowns para evitar rate limits, e rastreia custos. Nenhum workflow escolhe chaves manualmente вҖ” tudo Г© transparente.

## Dataset System

Sistema separado que pesquisa imagens reais em arquivos pГәblicos para usar nos vГӯdeos. Em vez de gerar tudo com IA, procuramos fotos reais da Г©poca. Actualmente integrado com Library of Congress, Europeana, Smithsonian e Wikimedia.

- **Language support:** PT/IT get special formatting (periodвҶ’semicolon for TTS naturalness)
- **Error handling:** Batch-level failures reported; all-or-nothing model

### Missing / Opportunities (from 03_PIPELINE_MAP.md)
- вң… Script cleaning (script_processor.py exists)
- вң… Scene division (parallel_screenplay.py exists)
- вқҢ **Scene bible aggregation** вҖ” Only partially automated (script_processor handles, but scene_bible_builder is listed as agent but code not found)
- вқҢ **Scene validation** (semantic, length balance, narrative coherence)
- вқҢ **Continuity extraction** (character names, props, locations per scene вҖ” for downstream B3+)
- вқҢ **Character identification** at scene level (currently done later in B3)
- вқҢ **Context preservation** (themes, tones, emotional beats per scene)

## Data Contracts (from Pipeline Map)

### Input to B1
```json
{
  "script": "Raw text from Google Sheet",
  "account_code": "003_wwii",
  "language": "en",
  "video_context": {
    "title": "...",
    "theme": "...",
    "channel_style": "..."
  }
}
```

### Output from B1 (scene_bible.json structure expected)
```json
{
  "video_id": "string",
  "script_raw": "string",
  "scenes": [
    {
      "scene_num": 1,
      "text": "scene text (polished for TTS)",
      "word_count": number,
      "duration_estimate_sec": number,
      "characters_mentioned": ["Name1", "Name2"],
      "locations": ["Location1"],
      "props": ["Prop1"],
      "emotional_tone": "string",
      "narrative_beat": "string"
    }
  ],
  "metadata": {
    "total_word_count": number,
    "total_scenes": number,
    "language": "string",
    "account": "string"
  }
}
```

## Key Observations

1. **The screenplay splitting logic exists but is monolithic** вҖ” parallel_screenplay.py mixes KMS rotation, batch orchestration, and GPT calls. For OpenClaw, this should be decomposed.

2. **Scene bible output structure is NOT formalized** вҖ” The v3 pipeline produces individual scenes but doesn't aggregate into a structured scene_bible.json. B1 needs to own this contract.

3. **Continuity extraction is missing** вҖ” B3 (Visual Planning) will need character/location/prop lists per scene. B1 should extract and structure this.

4. **Language-specific formatting happens at TTS, not at screenplay division** вҖ” PT/IT formatting is in parallel_tts.py, not parallel_screenplay.py. B1 should be agnostic; B2 (Audio) handles that.

5. **Validation is minimal** вҖ” Only word count checking. No semantic validation, no tone consistency, no narrative arc checking.

6. **Error recovery is all-or-nothing** вҖ” If one batch fails, entire screenplay fails. No graceful degradation.

## Structured Data Contracts Needed for B1

1. **SceneInput** вҖ” What B1 receives
2. **Scene** вҖ” Individual scene structure (as above)
3. **SceneBible** вҖ” Complete scene collection for video
4. **ContinuityExtract** вҖ” Characters, locations, props per scene (for B3)

# Branch 1 Pre-Production вҖ” Concrete Tools & Skills Worth Building

## Principle
Focus on **decomposable, reusable, cheap-model-friendly** tools that B1 agents can call.
NOT monolithic scripts. NOT full pipelines. Individual, testable skills.

---

## SKILL 1: Script Fetcher + Validator

### Purpose
Acquire raw script from external source (Google Sheet, API, file) and perform initial validation.

### Current State
- Orchestrator_NNN.py fetches from Google Sheet + atomically locks (status=processing)
- Basic whitespace cleaning exists (script_processor.py)

### OpenClaw Skill Needed: `script-intake`
**Model:** Haiku (cheap, validation-only)  
**Tools needed:**
- Google Sheets API integration (OAuth)
- File system read/write
- Locking mechanism (simple JSON lock file in shared/)

**Input:**
```json
{
  "source": "google_sheet",
  "sheet_id": "...",
  "script_row_id": "...",
  "account_code": "003_wwii"
}
```

**Output:**
```json
{
  "script_raw": "...",
  "metadata": {
    "source": "...",
    "fetched_at": "ISO8601",
    "word_count": number,
    "length_category": "short|medium|long"
  },
  "validation": {
    "is_valid": true,
    "errors": [],
    "warnings": []
  }
}
```

**Why skill?** Reusable for all videos. Isolates Google Sheets dependency. Can be called repeatedly if fetch fails.

---

## SKILL 2: Scene Splitter + Scene Bible Builder

### Purpose
Divide script into scenes using LLM, validate, and structure into scene_bible.json.

### Current State
- parallel_screenplay.py does batch splitting via GPT-4.1-mini
- Output: raw JSON list of scenes
- Scene bible aggregation: incomplete

### OpenClaw Skill Needed: `scene-splitter`
**Model:** GPT-4.1-mini (via OpenRouter, $0.15/M tokens) or Gemini Flash Lite (free)  
**Orchestration:** Decomposed into 2 skills:
1. **scene-splitter** вҖ” Takes script + constraints, returns scene list (KMS rotation handled by OpenClaw/Anthropic API)
2. **scene-bible-builder** вҖ” Aggregates + validates scenes, outputs final JSON

**Skill 1: scene-splitter**

Input:
```json
{
  "script": "raw text",
  "language": "en|pt|es|fr|it|de",
  "constraints": {
    "min_words_per_scene": 50,
    "max_words_per_scene": 200,
    "preferred_scene_count": 10,
    "batch_count": 3
  },
  "account_config": {
    "account_code": "003_wwii",
    "style_guidelines": "..."
  }
}
```

Output:
```json
{
  "scenes": [
    {
      "scene_num": 1,
      "text": "...",
      "word_count": 145,
      "batch_assignment": 1
    }
  ],
  "metadata": {
    "total_scenes": 10,
    "total_words": 1450,
    "language": "en",
    "processing_time_sec": 12.5
  }
}
```

**Skill 2: scene-bible-builder**

Input (output from scene-splitter):
```json
{
  "script_raw": "...",
  "scenes": [...],
  "language": "en",
  "account_code": "003_wwii"
}
```

Output:
```json
{
  "video_id": "unique_id",
  "script_raw": "...",
  "scenes": [
    {
      "scene_num": 1,
      "text": "...",
      "word_count": 145,
      "duration_estimate_sec": 18,
      "characters_mentioned": ["Napoleon"],
      "locations": ["Paris"],
      "props": [],
      "emotional_tone": "tense",
      "narrative_beat": "introduction"
    }
  ],
  "metadata": {
    "total_word_count": 1450,
    "total_scenes": 10,
    "language": "en",
    "account": "003_wwii",
    "created_at": "ISO8601"
  }
}
```

**Why split into 2 skills?** 
- scene-splitter is testable independently
- scene-bible-builder can retry if aggregation fails
- scene-splitter can be benchmarked against GPT-4.1-mini/Haiku/Flash Lite for cost-quality tradeoff

---

## SKILL 3: Continuity Extractor

### Purpose
Extract and structure characters, locations, props, and continuity information from scenes.
**Not currently done in v3 вҖ” B3 (Visual Planning) will need this.**

### OpenClaw Skill Needed: `continuity-extractor`
**Model:** Haiku or Flash Lite (cheap, structured output)

Input:
```json
{
  "scene_bible": {
    "scenes": [...],
    "language": "en"
  }
}
```

Output:
```json
{
  "characters": {
    "Napoleon": {
      "scenes_appeared": [1, 2, 3, 5],
      "aliases": ["Bonaparte", "Emperor"],
      "description": "French military leader",
      "visual_hints": ["uniform", "hat"]
    }
  },
  "locations": {
    "Paris": {
      "scenes_appeared": [1, 2],
      "era_context": "Early 1800s",
      "visual_hints": ["church", "street"]
    }
  },
  "props": {
    "sword": {
      "scenes_appeared": [3, 5],
      "significance": "strategic"
    }
  },
  "continuity_notes": [
    {
      "scene_from": 1,
      "scene_to": 2,
      "note": "Napoleon leaves Paris; appears in battlefield next"
    }
  ]
}
```

**Why skill?** Reusable, cacheable, improves downstream B3 (Visual Planning) quality.

---

## SKILL 4: Scene Validator + QA

### Purpose
Validate scene structure, word balance, narrative coherence, and tone consistency.

### OpenClaw Skill Needed: `scene-qa`
**Model:** Haiku (validation-only)

Input:
```json
{
  "scene_bible": {...},
  "validation_rules": {
    "check_word_balance": true,
    "check_narrative_arc": true,
    "check_tone_consistency": true
  }
}
```

Output:
```json
{
  "is_valid": true,
  "issues": [],
  "warnings": [
    {
      "type": "word_balance",
      "scene_num": 3,
      "message": "Scene 3 is 95 words, significantly shorter than average (145 words). May feel rushed.",
      "severity": "medium"
    }
  ],
  "score": {
    "narrative_coherence": 0.92,
    "tone_consistency": 0.85,
    "pacing": 0.88,
    "overall": 0.88
  }
}
```

---

## SKILL 5: Language-Specific Polish (PT/IT TTS Prep)

### Purpose
Apply language-specific formatting for TTS naturalness (currently in parallel_tts.py, phase 1).
**Move this upstream to B1** вҖ” scene_bible should include pre-polished text.

### OpenClaw Skill Needed: `tts-text-polish`
**Model:** Haiku (deterministic, rule-based)

Input:
```json
{
  "scenes": [...],
  "language": "pt|it"
}
```

Output:
```json
{
  "scenes": [
    {
      "scene_num": 1,
      "text_raw": "Original text.",
      "text_polished": "Original text;  (formatted for TTS)"
    }
  ]
}
```

**Why skill?** Isolates language-specific logic. Reusable for any TTS backend. Allows testing of polish rules.

---

## SKILL 6: Scene Context Enricher

### Purpose
Enrich each scene with metadata: estimated duration, emotional beats, narrative function, visual tone hints.
**Supports downstream B3 (Visual Planning) to make better decisions.**

### OpenClaw Skill Needed: `scene-enricher`
**Model:** GPT-4.1-mini or Flash Lite (creative, structured)

Input:
```json
{
  "scene_bible": {...},
  "account_config": {
    "style_guide": "...",
    "tone": "dark|light|dramatic|educational"
  }
}
```

Output:
```json
{
  "scenes": [
    {
      "scene_num": 1,
      "text": "...",
      "enrichment": {
        "duration_estimate_sec": 18,
        "narrative_function": "exposition|rising_action|climax|resolution",
        "emotional_beat": "tense|calm|exciting|sad",
        "visual_tone": "dark|bright|vintage|modern",
        "visual_suggestions": ["black and white", "dramatic lighting"],
        "pacing_note": "slower reveal, build tension",
        "character_focus": ["Napoleon"],
        "location_focus": ["Paris"],
        "imagery_hints": ["maps", "uniforms", "battle flags"]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**Why skill?** Makes B3 (Visual Planning) smarter. Reduces prompt engineering in downstream branches. Cacheable and reusable.

---

## Integration Flow (B1 вҶ’ B2 вҶ’ B3)

```
script-intake (fetch)
    вҶ“
scene-splitter (divide + batch)
    вҶ“
scene-bible-builder (aggregate + structure)
    вҶ“
continuity-extractor (extract chars/locs/props)
    вҶ“
scene-qa (validate structure)
    вҶ“
tts-text-polish (language-specific for B2)
    вҶ“
scene-enricher (context for B3)
    вҶ“
scene_bible.json + continu

# Branch 1 Tools & Skills вҖ” Concrete Recommendations

## OpenClaw Skills Worth Building

### Tier 1: Critical (Must-have for B1 to function)

#### Skill 1: `scene-bible-generator`
**Purpose:** Accept raw script, output structured scene_bible.json  
**Scope:** Decompose parallel_screenplay.py into OpenClaw-friendly skill  
**What it does:**
- Takes raw script + account config + video context
- Calls screenplay agent (GPT-4.1-mini via KMS rotation)
- Validates scene boundaries and word counts
- Extracts continuity data (characters, locations, props)
- Outputs structured JSON with strong schema validation

**Input Contract:**
```json
{
  "script_raw": "string",
  "account_code": "string",
  "language": "string",
  "scene_word_targets": {"min": 50, "max": 250},
  "video_context": {"title": "...", "theme": "..."}
}
```

**Output Contract:**
```json
{
  "scene_bible": {
    "video_id": "uuid",
    "scenes": [{
      "num": 1,
      "text": "polished text",
      "word_count": 120,
      "entities": {
        "characters": ["Name1", "Name2"],
        "locations": ["Loc1"],
        "props": ["Item1"]
      },
      "narrative_beat": "exposition|rising_action|climax|resolution",
      "tone": "somber|triumphant|mysterious"
    }],
    "continuity_extract": {...}
  },
  "validation_report": {...}
}
```

**Model:** GPT-4.1-mini (cheap, screenplay is commodity task)  
**Rate limits:** Semaphore(8) for batch processing  
**Error handling:** Graceful fallback for single-scene failures  
**Integration:** Reads from queue/Google Sheet via dedicated script_fetcher agent

---

#### Skill 2: `script-validator`
**Purpose:** Catch issues before screenplay division  
**What it does:**
- Detects language
- Validates character encoding (special chars that break TTS)
- Checks for placeholder text (e.g., "[DESCRIBE_SCENE]")
- Flags potential narrative issues (huge jumps between scenes, tense shifts)
- Suggests corrections without modifying original

**Model:** GPT-4.1-nano or Haiku (validation is cheap)  
**Output:** JSON report with severity + suggestions

---

#### Skill 3: `continuity-extractor`
**Purpose:** Pull structured entity lists for downstream branches  
**What it does:**
- Parses scene_bible.json
- Extracts all characters (with first mention scene)
- Extracts all locations (with appearance timeline)
- Extracts props and their context
- Cross-references aliases (e.g., "President Lincoln" = "Lincoln" = "Honest Abe")
- Outputs JSON registry for B3 (Visual Planning) and B4 (Asset Research)

**Model:** Haiku or local Ollama (regex + structured extraction)  
**Output:** JSON with character_registry, location_registry, prop_registry + scene cross-refs

---

### Tier 2: Valuable (Nice-to-have, increases quality)

#### Skill 4: `narrative-arc-analyzer`
**Purpose:** Validate pacing and emotional beats  
**What it does:**
- Analyzes scene sequence for narrative coherence
- Detects pacing issues (scenes too similar, no variety)
- Flags emotional arc flatness
- Suggests scene reordering if needed

**Model:** GPT-4.1-mini (needs semantic understanding)  
**Output:** Arc analysis + warnings

---

#### Skill 5: `scene-rebalancer`
**Purpose:** Auto-fix uneven scene lengths  
**What it does:**
- Detects outliers (scenes >350 words or <30 words)
- Suggests splits/merges
- If approved, regenerates affected scenes

**Model:** GPT-4.1-mini  
**Dependencies:** scene-bible-generator

---

#### Skill 6: `language-formatter`
**Purpose:** Apply language-specific TTS optimizations at screenplay level  
**What it does:**
- PT/IT: periodвҶ’semicolon conversions (for XTTS naturalness)
- CJK: character-level segmentation hints
- EN: contraction expansion check
- ES/FR: accent preservation validation

**Model:** Haiku or regex-based (deterministic rules)

---

## Integration Points with Existing v3 System

### Hook into parallel_screenplay.py
- **Where:** CentralizedKeyClient initialization
- **What:** Replace the monolithic parallel processing with OpenClaw skill invocation
- **How:** B1 agent calls scene-bible-generator skill вҶ’ skill wraps KMS logic вҶ’ returns JSON
- **Benefit:** Clear separation of concerns, testable, reusable

### Hook into KMS (Key Management System)
- **What:** KMS already exists and is used by v3
- **How:** scene-bible-generator skill should use existing KMS client
- **Key:** CentralizedKeyClient is async; OpenClaw may need wrapper for syncвҶ’async bridging

### Hook into Google Sheet polling
- **What:** script_fetcher agent needs to poll for new scripts
- **Recommendation:** Keep Google Sheets API integration in a separate "script_source" adapter
- **B1 skill:** Receives already-fetched script JSON, doesn't own Sheet integration

---

## Not Recommended as Skills (Keep in Agent Prompts Instead)

### Why not skill: "screenplay-divider"
- Too tightly coupled to GPT-4.1-mini specific prompts
- Account-specific prompts vary widely (WWII vs dark history vs sci-fi)
- Better as dynamic prompt in agent's instruction set, not hardcoded skill

### Why not skill: "scene-formatter"
- Language rules change frequently
- Coupled to TTS provider (Gemini vs ElevenLabs vs Elevenlabs have different quirks)
- Better owned by B2 (Audio branch) since it's TTS-specific

---

## Recommended OpenClaw Tools (Not Skills)

### Tool 1: KMS Client Wrapper
**Purpose:** Async KMS key management for screenplay agents  
**What it does:**
- Wraps existing CentralizedKeyClient
- Exposes simple `get_key(provider, model)` interface
- Handles rotation, cooldowns, cost tracking
- Logs to OpenClaw heartbeat for monitoring

**Integration:** scene-bible-generator skill uses this

---

### Tool 2: Script Source Adapter
**Purpose:** Fetch scripts from multiple sources (Google Sheet, queue, webhook)  
**What it does:**
- Abstract script fetching
- Supports Google Sheets, local JSON files, Telegram messages
- Returns normalized ScriptInput schema
- Caches to avoid redundant fetches

---

### Tool 3: Validation Report Formatter
**Purpose:** Normalize validation output for B1вҶ’B2вҶ’B3 handoff  
**What it does:**
- Takes raw validation JSON
- Formats for human readability + machine parsing
- Logs to Telegram + returns to PM

## Why Structured Artifacts?

Branch 1 is the _gateway_ вҖ” it ingests raw data and structures it. Everything downstream (B2-B8) depends on the **quality, schema consistency, and completeness** of B1 output.

In the current v3 pipeline, B1 output is loosely structured. For OpenClaw agent hierarchy, we need **explicit, validated contracts** that:
1. Enable clear error boundaries
2. Allow downstream agents to validate input before processing
3. Support monitoring and quality gates
4. Enable easy schema evolution

---

- Log validation errors to Telegram + OpenClaw heartbeat

### At Read Time (B2-B9)
- Lightweight schema version check
- Fallback to known compatible version if minor mismatch
- Error if major version mismatch (requires B1 regeneration)

### Tooling
- **Pydantic:** Python validation (if B1 agents are Python)
- **JSON Schema:** Language-agnostic, enforces at API boundary
- **OpenAPI:** If exposing artifact queries via REST

## Critical Contracts for Downstream

**B2 (Audio) Expects:**
- scene_bible.json with complete, validated scene text
- No empty scene.text fields
- All word_count fields populated

**B3 (Visual Planning) Expects:**
- continuity_extract.json with full character/location/prop lists
- scene_bible.json with narrative beats and visual_hints
- Valid character names and location names

**B4 (Asset Research) Expects:**

**B6 (Scene Composition) Expects:**
- scene_bible.json with transitions.from_previous and transitions.to_next

- Timing data (estimated_duration_sec) for sync with audio

---

## Governance Notes

1. **B1 is the schema owner** вҖ” any changes to scene_bible.json schema require B1 agent approval
2. **Backward compatibility first** вҖ” try to never break downstream
3. **Monitoring watches schemas** вҖ” B10 (Monitoring) tracks schema versions in use across all videos
4. **Clear error messages** вҖ” when downstream agents find malformed artifacts, error should point to B1 for remediation

# Branch 1 Cheap Model Workflow

## Goal
Process scripts through B1 (Pre-Production) at minimal cost while maintaining quality for downstream branches.

**Target cost:** $0.10-0.30 per video for full B1 pipeline (screenplay division + validation + continuity extraction)

---

## Model Selection Matrix

### Task 1: Script Validation (Before Division)

**Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5 (via Anthropic API)  
**Cost:** ~$0.005 per validation  
**Why Haiku:**
- Lightweight validation (character encoding, placeholders, sanity checks)
- Fast inference
- Reliable for structured checks
- Cheap compared to GPT-4

**Input Token Budget:** ~500 tokens (script + validation rules)  
**Output Token Budget:** ~300 tokens (JSON report)  
**Cost per call:** (500 + 300) Г— $0.80/$1M = ~$0.0006 (Haiku pricing)

---

### Task 2: Screenplay Division (Core Work)

**Model:** GPT-4.1-mini (via KMS OpenAI fallback)  
**Cost:** ~$0.08-0.12 per video  
**Why GPT-4.1-mini:**
- Existing in v3 pipeline (proven prompts, tuning)
- Handles scene splitting better than cheaper models
- Balances cost vs quality
- Managed via KMS (key rotation, cooldown handling)

**Input Token Budget:** ~1500 tokens (script + video context + constraints)  
**Output Token Budget:** ~800 tokens (JSON scenes)  
**Cost per call:** (1500 + 800) Г— $0.15/$1M = ~$0.000345 Г— 2 batches = ~$0.0007 per batch

**Batching Strategy:**
- Split long scripts into 2-4 batches (parallel processing via Semaphore(8))
- Each batch ~800 words
- Total screenplay cost: ~$0.08-0.10 per video

---

### Task 3: Continuity Extraction (Lightweight)

**Model:** Claude Haiku 4.5 or local Ollama  
**Cost:** ~$0.005-0.01 per video  
**Why Haiku/Ollama:**
- Entity extraction is rule-based + light semantic understanding
- No complex reasoning needed
- Haiku is fast and cheap

**Alternative:** Use local Ollama with Qwen-3.5 (free, instant)

**Input:** scene_bible.json (structured data)  
**Output:** continuity_extract.json (entity registry)  
**Cost if Haiku:** ~$0.008  
**Cost if Ollama:** $0

---

### Task 4: Narrative Arc Validation (Optional, Conditional)

**Model:** GPT-4.1-mini (only if quality score < 0.80)  
**Cost:** ~$0.03 (conditional execution)  
**Why conditional:**
- Most scripts don't need this check
- Only trigger if script_validator warns about pacing issues
- Reduces average cost

---

## Orchestration Workflow (Haiku as Coordinator)

Use a cheap coordinator agent (Haiku) to decide which tasks to run:

```
START
  в”ңв”Җ Haiku: Quick validation check (~$0.005)
  в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җ If PASS вҶ’ proceed
  в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ If FAIL вҶ’ halt, report to PM
  в”Ӯ
  в”ңв”Җ GPT-4.1-mini: Screenplay division (~$0.10)
  в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ Output: raw scene list
  в”Ӯ
  в”ңв”Җ Haiku: Continuity extraction (~$0.008)
  в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ Output: character/location registry
  в”Ӯ
  в”ңв”Җ Haiku: Quality scoring (~$0.005)
  в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җ If score >= 0.85 вҶ’ done (cost: $0.118)
  в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ If score < 0.85 вҶ’ escalate to GPT-4.1-mini arc check (~$0.03)
  в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җ Aggregate вҶ’ scene_bible.json, continuity_extract.json
```

**Average cost per video:** $0.118 (happy path) to $0.148 (with arc check)

---

## Budget Allocation for B1

### Monthly Volume Assumption
- **40 videos/week** Г— 4 weeks = **160 videos/month**

### Cost Breakdown

| Task | Cost/Video | 160/month | Monthly |
|------|-----------|-----------|---------|
| Script validation | $0.005 | 160 | $0.80 |
| Screenplay division | $0.10 | 160 | $16.00 |
| Continuity extraction | $0.008 | 160 | $1.28 |
| Quality check (Haiku) | $0.005 | 160 | $0.80 |
| Arc validation (30% of vids) | $0.03 | 48 | $1.44 |
| **Subtotal (B1 tasks)** | **$0.148** | **160** | **$20.32** |
| **KMS overhead** | ~5% | - | **$1.02** |
| **TOTAL B1** | - | - | **~$21.34** |

---

## Model Routing Rules

### If KMS GPT-4.1-mini Fails
**Fallback chain:**
1. GPT-4.1-mini via OpenRouter ($0.15/1M tokens, same cost)
2. Claude Sonnet via Anthropic API ($3/$15 per 1M tokens) вҖ” more expensive
3. Gemini 2.5 Flash via KMS (free tier)

**Decision logic:**
- Try KMS first (cost: $0.10)
- If rate-limited, retry with exponential backoff (KMS handles internally)
- If KMS exhausted, fall back to OpenRouter (cost: same, slower)
- Log fallback to B10 (Monitoring)

---

## Optimization Strategies

### Strategy 1: Batch Validation Across Multiple Videos
Instead of validating 1 script at a time, batch 10 scripts in single Haiku call:
- Input: 10 scripts
- Cost: ~$0.03 instead of $0.05 (saves $0.02 = 40% reduction)
- Trade-off: Latency (must wait for all 10)

**When to use:** Overnight batch processing, not urgent videos

---

### Strategy 2: Skip Optional Checks for High-Confidence Scripts
- Track script quality score over time per account
- If account has >95% pass rate, skip arc validation for routine scripts
- Still validate occasionally (2-3 per week) to catch drift

**Savings:** ~$0.03 per video on high-confidence accounts = ~$5/month

---

### Strategy 3: Local Continuation Extraction (Ollama)
Replace Haiku continuity extraction with local Ollama:
- Model: Qwen-3.5-9B (already available on M1)
- Cost: $0 (local inference)
- Speed: 2-3 sec per extraction
- Trade-off: ~5% less accuracy in alias detection

**Savings:** ~$0.008 per video = ~$1.28/month

**Breakeven:** If one Ollama inference fails per 100 videos, cost of re-running with Haiku = $0.008 (1% failure, 1 cost). Not worth it unless failures <1%.

**Recommendation:** Keep Haiku; Ollama as fallback for high-volume overflow.

---

## Cost Sensitivity Analysis

### If Video Volume Doubles (80 videos/week вҶ’ 160/week)
- **Current cost:** $21.34/month (160 videos)
- **New cost:** $42.68/month (320 videos)
- **Per-video cost:** stays at $0.148

- **Haiku decreases 50%:** -$2/month (minimal impact, Haiku is already cheap)
- **KMS keys exhaust:** Fallback to OpenRouter (no cost change, just slower)

### If Validation Complexity Increases

- Add dialogue validation: +$0.03 with GPT-4.1-mini
- **Total could reach $0.20-0.25 per video**

script_validator (Haiku) вҶ’ screenplay_divider (GPT-4.1-mini) вҶ’ continuity_extractor (Haiku)

## Emergency Cost Controls

If costs spike unexpectedly:

1. **Haiku quota exceeded:** Switch to Ollama for extraction (free)
2. **GPT-4.1-mini overrun:** Use Gemini Flash fallback via KMS (free tier)
3. **All APIs failing:** Queue scripts for next day (batch processing)
4. **Emergency escalation:** Disable arc validation, skip non-critical videos

**Cost ceiling:** $50/month for B1 (hard limit before escalation)

## Long-Term Optimization Paths

- Cost: $50 one-time (Ollama training)

### Path 2: Hybrid Extraction
- Use regex + Ollama for 80% of extractions (fast, free)

- Cost: $0.0004 per video

- If using same system prompt for 100+ videos, use OpenAI prompt caching

- Haiku struggles with complex scene splitting

# Branch 1 Implementation Roadmap

## Executive Summary

**Status:** Pre-production phase complete. Ready for agent skeleton + skill buildout.

**Scope:** Branch 1 (Pre-Production) as entry point to Content Factory agent system

**Duration:** 4 weeks to MVP (weeks 1-4), then incremental optimization

**Deliverables by Week:**

- Week 2: Validation + quality gating
- Week 3: Continuity extraction + optimization
- Week 4: Integration testing + handoff to B2

---

## Week-by-Week Plan

### WEEK 1: Foundation & MVP Skills

**Goal:** Get B1 agent running with screenplay division skill, basic schema

**Deliverables:**

1. **B1 Agent Skeleton**
   - Folder: `auto_content_factory/agents/branches/01_pre_production/`
   - Files:
     - `IDENTITY.md` (B1 Branch Manager role)
     - `CONTEXT.md` (how B1 fits in hierarchy, data flow)
     - `RULES.md` (error handling, escalation paths)
     - `MEMORY.md` (learnings, issues)

2. **Skill 1: `screenplay-division-skill`** вӯҗ CRITICAL
   - Input: ScriptInput JSON (script_raw + account + language)
   - Process: Wrap existing parallel_screenplay.py logic
   - Output: List of Scene objects
   - Model: GPT-4.1-mini via KMS
   - Error handling: Batch-level retry + logging
   - Testing: 5 test scripts (EN, PT, different genres)

3. **JSON Schemas (Pydantic + JSON Schema)**

   - Scene schema (basic version)
   - SceneOutput (list of scenes)
   - Store in: `auto_content_factory/shared/schemas/`

   - No screenplay logic yet
   - Error: Script not found вҶ’ escalate to B10

**Estimated effort:** 30-40 hours (Claude Code task)

**Testing:**
- Unit test screenplay-division-skill with 5 sample scripts
- Integration test script_fetcher + screenplay-division-skill
- Cost tracking: Log actual API costs for 5 videos

**Risks:**
- KMS integration might need tweaking for async
- Parallel_screenplay.py has assumptions about file paths
- Mitigation: Keep wrapper thin, call existing v3 code

---

### WEEK 2: Validation & Quality Gating

**Goal:** Add pre-flight checks + quality scoring before screenplay division

**Deliverables:**

1. **Skill 2: `script-validator-skill`**
   - Input: ScriptInput
   - Checks:
     - Language detection (should match declared language)
     - Character encoding (special chars that break TTS)
     - Placeholder detection ("[DESCRIBE_SCENE]" etc.)
     - Word count bounds
     - Special character flagging (en-dashes, quotes)
   - Output: ValidationReport JSON
   - Model: Haiku 4.5
   - Cost: ~$0.005 per video

2. **Skill 3: `quality-scorer-skill`**
   - Input: Raw screenplay output (list of scenes)
   - Measures:
     - Scene length distribution
     - Narrative coherence (sentences per scene, readability)
     - Entity count reasonableness
     - Pacing issues (huge scene count variance)
   - Output: QualityScore (0-100) + detailed metrics
   - Model: Haiku 4.5
   - Cost: ~$0.005 per video

3. **Agent: `scene_bible_builder`**
   - Input: Screenplay (list of scenes) + validation report
   - Aggregates into SceneBible JSON
   - Enriches with metadata (video_id, timestamps)
   - Writes to `shared/scene_bible/{video_id}/scene_bible.json`
   - Error handling: Failed aggregation вҶ’ halt, report to PM

4. **Updated Schemas**
   - ValidationReport (full schema from 03_STRUCTURED_ARTIFACTS.md)
   - QualityScore + metrics
   - SceneBible (complete, from 03_STRUCTURED_ARTIFACTS.md)

5. **B1 Orchestrator Logic**
   - Agents run in sequence:
     - script_fetcher вҶ’ script_validator вҶ’ screenplay_divider
     - If validator fails: halt, report
     - If screenplay succeeds: proceed to quality_scorer
     - If quality_scorer < 0.80: flag for B2 review (optional arc check)
     - scene_bible_builder: aggregate + write artifact

6. **Monitoring Integration**
   - B1 agent logs:
     - Each screenplay call (tokens, cost, model, latency)
     - Validation passes/failures
     - Quality scores per video
   - Send to B10 (Monitoring) queue for aggregation

**Estimated effort:** 25-30 hours

**Testing:**
- Run 20 test scripts through full pipeline
- Measure cost: should be ~$0.02 per video (validation + quality)
- Check artifact writing: confirm JSON is valid and complete
- Quality score distribution: should be >0.85 for 80% of videos

**Risks:**
- Quality scorer might be too strict/lenient
- Mitigation: Fine-tune thresholds based on first 20 videos

---

### WEEK 3: Continuity Extraction & Optimization

**Goal:** Extract entity registry for downstream branches + optimize costs

**Deliverables:**

1. **Skill 4: `continuity-extractor-skill`** (HIGH VALUE)
   - Input: SceneBible JSON
   - Extracts:
     - All characters (with aliases, scenes, role)
     - All locations (with aliases, scenes, era)
     - All props (with scenes, significance)
     - Timeline info (era, historical context)
   - Output: ContinuityExtract JSON (from 03_STRUCTURED_ARTIFACTS.md)
   - Model: Haiku 4.5 (preferred) or local Ollama (optional)
   - Cost: $0.008 (Haiku) or $0 (Ollama)
   - Quality: Haiku >= 95% accuracy on entity extraction

2. **Agent: `continuity_extractor`**
   - Simple wrapper around skill
   - Writes to `shared/scene_bible/{video_id}/continuity_extract.json`
   - Validation: check schema
   - Error: Failed extraction вҶ’ re-run once with Haiku if tried Ollama

3. **Optimizations Implemented**
   - **Batch validation:** Option to validate 5+ scripts in one Haiku call
   - **Cost tracking:** B1 agent logs cost per component
   - **Caching:** If same account config, reuse screenplay context (KMS caching)
   - **Fallback testing:** Verify fallback chain works (KMS вҶ’ OpenRouter)

4. **B1 Full Orchestration**
   ```
   script_fetcher
     вҶ“
   script_validator (Haiku)
     в”ңв”Җ Pass? Continue
     в””в”Җ Fail? Halt + report
     вҶ“
   screenplay_divider (GPT-4.1-mini via KMS) [BATCH UP TO 8]
     вҶ“
   quality_scorer (Haiku)
     в”ңв”Җ Pass (>0.85)? вҶ’ Continue
     в””в”Җ Fail? вҶ’ Arc validator (GPT-4.1-mini) [CONDITIONAL]
     вҶ“
   scene_bible_builder
     вҶ“
   continuity_extractor (Haiku or Ollama)
     вҶ“
   Write artifacts:
     - shared/scene_bible/{video_id}/scene_bible.json
     - shared/scene_bible/{video_id}/continuity_extract.json
     - shared/scene_bible/{video_id}/validation_report.json
   ```

5. **Documentation**
   - B1 agent handbook (how to troubleshoot)
   - Schema documentation (for downstream branches)
   - Cost breakdown report (weekly)

**Estimated effort:** 20-25 hours

**Testing:**
- Full end-to-end test: 30 videos across 3 accounts
- Cost verification: Should be ~$0.13-0.15 per video
- Artifact validation: Check JSON schema compliance
- Downstream readiness: B2/B3 test reading artifacts

**Performance targets:**
- Screenplay division: 2-3 min per video (4x batches parallel)
- Full B1 pipeline: 5-7 min per video end-to-end
- Cost: $0.13-0.15 per video (happy path)
- Failure rate: <2% (one video per 50)

---

### WEEK 4: Integration & Handoff

**Goal:** Production readiness + handoff to B2 (Audio)

**Deliverables:**

1. **Production Checklist**
   - вң… All agents have IDENTITY + CONTEXT + RULES
   - вң… Schema validation enforced at artifact write
   - вң… Error handling tested (KMS failures, malformed scripts)
   - вң… Cost monitoring live (B10 integration)
   - вң… Fallback chains tested
   - вң… Documentation complete

2. **B1вҶ”B2 Integration**
   - B2 (Audio) reads scene_bible.json
   - B2 validates scene_bible schema before processing
   - Test: B2 can process 5 scripts from B1 without errors
   - Handoff meeting: B1 вҶ’ B2 agent sync on artifact format

3. **Monitoring Dashboard**
   - B10 (Monitoring) collects B1 metrics:
     - Videos processed (daily)
     - Cost per video (rolling 7-day average)
     - Failure rate (by error type)
     - Quality score distribution
   - Dashboard: Simple JSON report in `shared/monitoring/b1_daily_report.json`

4. **Runbook**
   - Troubleshooting guide: "Screenplay failed" вҶ’ steps to diagnose
   - Escalation paths: When to stop B1 and report to PM
   - Cost alerts: When to activate emergency cost controls

5. **Optional: Skill Improvements (if time)**
   - **Skill 5: `scene-rebalancer-skill`** (nice-to-have)
     - Auto-fix outlier scene lengths
     - Re-runs screenplay division on flagged scenes
     - Cost: ~$0.02 per video (only if needed)
   
   - **Skill 6: `narrative-arc-validator-skill`** (nice-to-have)
     - Validate pacing, emotional arc
     - Trigger only if quality_scorer < 0.80
     - Model: GPT-4.1-mini
     - Cost: ~$0.03 (conditional)

**Estimated effort:** 15-20 hours

**Testing:**
- End-to-end test with production volume (100 videos)
- Load test: 10 videos simultaneously (async stress test)
- Fallback recovery: Simulate KMS failure, verify fallback to OpenRouter
- Cost forecast: Extrapolate monthly cost at current volume

   - Schema v1.0 with version control
   - Immutable after handoff; read by all downstream branches (B2-B9)

2. **continuity_extract.json** (5-10 KB per video)
   - Lightweight entity registry (characters, locations, props)
   - Cross-references per scene
   - Optimized for B3/B4 queries ("all locations in scene 5?")

3. **script_validation_report.json**
   - Pre-division quality gate
   - Checks: encoding, placeholders, language detection, narrative structure
   - Used by B1 to decide whether to proceed or halt

**Storage:** `auto_content_factory/shared/scene_bible/{video_id}/`

**Governance:** B1 is schema owner; backward compatibility first; versioning required for evolution.

### 3. Cheap Model Workflow вң…

**Recommended model routing:**

| Task | Model | Cost | Why |
|------|-------|------|-----|
| Script validation | Haiku 4.5 | $0.005 | Lightweight checks |
| Screenplay division | GPT-4.1-mini (KMS) | $0.10 | Proven, balanced |

- Skip arc validation for high-confidence accounts = $0.03 savings per video
